paragraph: Excavations at the Veltharn Basin site (41°33'N, 28°54'E) conducted between 2021 and 2024 under the direction of Dr. Cassivara Olendt of the Prussenwald Institute for Archaeological Sciences have uncovered a densely stratified settlement belonging to the Thovari culture, active during the late Bronze Age transitional period approximately 1180–870 BCE. The team recovered 347 ceramic vessels across three principal occupation horizons at depths ranging from 42 cm to 218 cm below present surface grade. Notably, 61 of these vessels bear a distinctive burnished red slip accompanied by impressed geometric registers previously unattested in the broader Thovari corpus. Radiocarbon dating of carbonized grain assemblages from Floor Horizon II returned a calibrated age range of 1043–928 BCE (2-sigma, OxCal 4.4). The site's spatial organization, with a central plaza measuring 34 m × 27 m flanked by parallel storage-hall complexes, suggests a degree of urban planning consistent with a regional administrative node. Faunal remains indicate heavy reliance on caprid husbandry alongside moderate exploitation of aurochs-derived cattle, with isotopic ratios (delta-13C: -19.4 per mil; delta-15N: 8.7 per mil) suggesting pasturing in mixed woodland-steppe environments. A full site report is forthcoming in the Veltharn Basin Monograph Series.

paragraph: The Korov-Drelm necropolis (57°08'N, 63°41'E), first identified via aerial thermographic survey in 2018, has yielded 112 inhumation burials following three field seasons of systematic excavation directed by Dr. Ferenka Bulscht of the Orvanna University Centre for Funerary Studies. The burials, arranged in concentric arcs around a central earthen mound 8.3 m in height, date broadly to 620–490 BCE on the basis of 18 AMS radiocarbon measurements. Grave goods include bronze fibulae (n=74), iron shaft-hole axes (n=31), amber disc pendants (n=88), and a remarkable series of 14 gilded antler headdresses, each averaging 41 cm in span. Metallurgical analysis of seven fibulae identified a tin-bronze alloy with mean tin content of 11.3 wt%, consistent with regional smelting traditions documented at the nearby Poltrevsk depot. The skeletal assemblage, studied by bioarchaeologist Dr. Mivna Throsser, records stature means of 171.4 cm for males (n=38) and 161.8 cm for females (n=42), with trauma patterns on six individuals suggesting interpersonal violence. Isotopic strontium data (87Sr/86Sr: 0.7098–0.7124) imply that at least 19 individuals were non-local in origin, raising questions about the necropolis's role as a supra-regional burial ground for the Drevali confederacy.

paragraph: Field campaigns undertaken during 2022 and 2023 at Qalat Borrhun (34°51'N, 40°17'E) by the joint Borrhun Archaeological Project, co-directed by Dr. Selveth Imran-Haase (Quervaine Institute of Near Eastern Archaeology) and Dr. Borva Khaltsev (University of Drakovar), have exposed a monumental gateway complex attributable to the Akheltarn state, ca. 880–740 BCE. The gateway, constructed of dressed limestone blocks averaging 1.2 m × 0.6 m × 0.4 m, incorporates two flanking towers each measuring 9.7 m × 8.3 m in plan. Cuneiform-adjacent inscriptions totaling 246 preserved signs on a basalt orthostat were recorded and are under analysis; preliminary paleographic comparison places the script within an Akheltarn administrative register distinct from the contemporaneous Harven syllabary. Associated destruction debris at 1.4 m depth contained 23 bronze arrowheads, fragments of at least four iron scale-armor coats, and a cache of 512 unbaked clay tablets bearing numerical notations, possibly inventory records. Charred wood from the destruction context returned calibrated radiocarbon dates of 793–761 BCE (1-sigma), consistent with historically attested inter-polity conflict within the middle Borrhun corridor. Geophysical magnetometry indicates additional unexcavated structures extending at least 180 m to the northeast, forming the probable administrative quarter of the settlement.

paragraph: Researchers from the Norveldt Centre for Pre-Columbian Studies, led by Dr. Anzelika Torrevs, have completed a four-season investigation of the Cuachma Terrace Complex (16°44'N, 92°18'W), a site attributable to the Taveltec civilization of highland Mesoamerica, dated approximately 350 BCE–210 CE. The complex encompasses 14 interconnected terraced platforms constructed of compacted earth and rubble-fill revetments, with the largest measuring 63 m × 47 m at base and rising 11.4 m above the surrounding alluvial plain. Stratigraphic excavation of Unit 7 revealed four superimposed floor surfaces of polished lime plaster, the earliest dating to 328–291 BCE by AMS radiocarbon on embedded charcoal (2-sigma). A sub-floor offering cache recovered from the primary construction phase contained 38 obsidian prismatic blades, 14 greenstone celts, and three complete Spondylus-like bivalve shells sourced, by portable XRF, to coastal volcanic deposit zones approximately 470 km to the southwest. Ceramic analysis identified 1,203 rim sherds representing a minimum of 94 vessel forms, with 17% bearing polychrome bichrome decoration unique to the Taveltec Middle Horizon assemblage. The terrace alignment, oriented 17° east of magnetic north, corresponds with a solstitial sunrise azimuth, suggesting calendrical significance in the complex's original design.

paragraph: A rescue excavation triggered by pipeline construction near Halveth Crossing (50°29'N, 14°37'E) in 2023 exposed a previously unknown Iron Age settlement belonging to the Lorvenni culture, excavated under emergency permit by Dr. Otmar Dreschke of the Mittelbogen Heritage Authority. The site, measuring at least 2.1 ha based on surface geophysics, yielded 18 semi-subterranean rectangular pit-structures with preserved post-hole patterns indicating gable-roofed superstructures. Pottery from the primary occupation layer (depth 55–140 cm) includes 4,712 sherds, of which 631 are of the finger-impressed corded variety diagnostic of the Lorvenni Phase III assemblage, dated by cross-correlation to approximately 480–380 BCE. Iron slag deposits totaling an estimated 34 kg were recovered from two dedicated smelting areas, and three bloom fragments retain sufficient structure for microstructural analysis. A concentration of 17 amber beads and two bronze torcs in Pit Structure 9 suggests the structure had special-function status, possibly linked to craft-elite activities. Seeds recovered by flotation (n=2,841) are dominated by emmer wheat and barley, with minor representation of lentil and flax, consistent with a mixed-agriculture subsistence base. Radiocarbon dates on short-lived seeds cluster tightly at 432–408 BCE (2-sigma, n=4 dates).

paragraph: The Dhal Provarna Survey Project, a systematic pedestrian and remote-sensing investigation of a 280 km² block in the Provarna uplands (27°19'N, 68°53'E), conducted between 2019 and 2024 under the direction of Dr. Wultha Bremsen (Kreyenveld Institute for South Asian Antiquity), has documented 143 previously unrecorded archaeological sites spanning the Chalcolithic through early historic periods. Among the most significant discoveries is the Provarna Ridge Settlement (PRsit-77), a walled enclosure 410 m × 295 m containing grid-planned streets and at least nine public-access tank features. Surface ceramics and a preliminary test trench to 3.7 m depth indicate occupation from approximately 2700 to 1800 BCE, potentially extending the known western range of the Dhal Horizon culture by 220 km. Lithic scatters at 34 upland locations suggest a robust seasonal mobile component to Dhal Horizon land use, with chert debitage sourcing by XRF indicating raw material transport distances of up to 310 km. The survey also identified 12 rock-art panels bearing pecked geometric and zoomorphic motifs; uranium-series minimum ages on overlying flowstone at two panels constrain production to before 3,100 ± 140 BP. A full publication of all 143 site records is in preparation for the Provarna Archaeological Corpus monograph.

paragraph: Underwater excavations at the Sulvan Harbour site (43°07'N, 28°41'E), conducted in 2022 and 2023 by the Maritime Heritage Foundation of Borethia under the direction of Dr. Ilmena Skavroth, have documented the remains of a Late Antiquity-period merchant vessel of the Velthric tradition, resting at 18.4 m depth on a sandy seabed. The hull, preserved to a length of 22.7 m and a maximum beam of 6.1 m, is constructed of oak with elm strakes and features a distinctive double-planked keel assembly previously unattested in Velthric shipbuilding. Dendrochronological sampling of 11 hull timbers yielded a felling date of 487 CE ± 3 years, placing construction at the close of the Velthric Third Maritime Phase. The cargo, partially intact, consists of 340 transport amphorae of Falveni Type IIb, 28 intact glass unguentaria, and 14 lead ingots each bearing stamped ownership marks in the Surannic script. A wooden writing tablet recovered from the stern cabin area preserves 34 legible lines of administrative text referencing port dues and a consignment of textile goods. Conservation of all recovered organic materials is ongoing at the Borethian Maritime Conservation Laboratory, with full analysis expected to be complete by late 2026.

paragraph: Dr. Pratheva Osundavi of the Tarankhel University Archaeological Institute led a three-season excavation (2020–2022) at Murham Mound (28°06'N, 72°44'E), uncovering evidence for a late phase of the Gurendi urban tradition spanning approximately 1650–1350 BCE. A craft-production quarter in the site's western sector yielded 44 crucible fragments with residues of copper-tin alloy, 127 waster beads of faience, and a stone mold for casting flat axes in a form consistent with the Gurendi Type 3 axe typology. Botanical analysis of 22 flotation samples identified 14 cultivated taxa, with wheat, millet, and sesame well-represented alongside minor quantities of cotton seed. Isotopic analysis of 19 human inhumations beneath domestic floor surfaces (delta-18O: -5.1 to -3.7 per mil) is consistent with local groundwater signatures, suggesting a sedentary resident population rather than seasonal migrants. The site's abandonment layer, rich in unburied human skeletal elements and fire-damaged architecture, hints at a rapid and violent site-end event.

paragraph: Archaeologists from the Vestrakken School of Cultural Heritage, led by Dr. Elva Nordenskar, have published findings from the Brekka Longhouse Cluster (68°14'N, 14°27'E), an early medieval complex attributed to the Gorvenni maritime community, dated by a suite of 22 radiocarbon measurements to 620–780 CE. The cluster comprises seven rectangular timber-framed longhouses, each with bowing-wall profile characteristic of Gorvenni domestic architecture, arranged along a terraced slope overlooking a now-silted estuary. Associated artifact assemblages include 614 iron nails, 38 whetstone fragments, 12 bone combs in various stages of manufacture, and a notable cache of 23 hack-silver pieces deposited beneath the threshold of the eastern entrance. Faunal analysis of 14,230 identified specimens indicates a heavily marine-oriented diet, with cod, haddock, and seal dominating the assemblage. Organic residue analysis of 47 ceramic vessels identified lipid signatures consistent with fish oil processing in 29 samples. The site appears to represent a seasonally occupied fishing and processing station connected to longer-distance exchange networks, as suggested by the presence of 8 steatite vessel fragments sourced by geochemical fingerprinting to quarries 340 km to the north.

paragraph: The Thalvara Archaeological Mission, jointly administered by the Lenthorst Institute of Classical Studies and the University of Verrakan, conducted its sixth field season in 2023 at Thalvara Lower Town (37°52'N, 27°14'E), exposing a substantial portion of a mid-first-millennium BCE civic complex attributable to the Phorenic city-states. A colonnaded stoa, 74.3 m in length and 12.8 m in depth, was traced over its full extent, with 22 column-base drums recovered in situ, each 0.98 m in diameter and carved from local blue-grey limestone. An associated drain system of terracotta pipes, with individual segments 0.62 m in length and internal diameter of 0.18 m, connects the stoa courtyard to a still-functional underground cistern with an estimated capacity of 1,400 m³. A sealed destruction deposit beneath the stoa portico, dated by associated ceramics and two AMS radiocarbon dates to 378–351 BCE, contained 14 fragmentary terracotta figurines, 88 bronze coins of five denominations, and three lead curse tablets inscribed with 212 total lines of text in the Phorenic syllabary. Numismatic analysis of the coins, conducted by Dr. Solveia Rannek, demonstrates a mint attribution to at least three separate city-states, indicative of wide commercial integration within the Phorenic league. Publication of the stoa excavation is scheduled for the Thalvara Studies series in 2025.

paragraph: A research team from the Kernaveth Institute for Amerindian Archaeology, directed by Dr. Quivaren Mosst, completed excavations in 2024 at the Chalveth Midden Complex (48°33'N, 123°44'W), a shell-midden site attributed to the Solveni coastal tradition and spanning approximately 3200–1800 BCE. The complex, encompassing an estimated 0.8 ha and reaching a maximum depth of 4.2 m, was sampled through 16 excavation units of 1 m × 1 m and four larger 2 m × 4 m trenches. Stratigraphic analysis identified nine discrete midden episodes, each separated by sterile sand lenses, suggesting episodic rather than continuous deposition. Shell assemblages are dominated by Mytilus-like mussels (62%) and a large barnacle species (18%), with minor contributions from sea-urchin, crab, and three species of gastropod. Fish bone analysis identified 23 taxa, with a large flatfish species and herring analogues most prevalent; size-at-age reconstructions indicate harvesting of adult individuals. A total of 1,104 lithic artifacts were recovered, including 88 ground-stone net-sinkers, 47 bone harpoon tips, and 14 antler wedges. A sub-midden burial, recovered from Unit 7 at 3.8 m depth, comprises the articulated remains of an adult male with an estimated age-at-death of 38–47 years, accompanied by a strung necklace of 143 perforated shell beads. AMS dating of the skeleton returned 2890–2710 BCE (2-sigma).

paragraph: Dr. Brenava Koltharn and colleagues from the Drexveld Centre for Eurasian Steppe Archaeology have published the results of a 2021–2023 investigation of the Tevlanka Kurgan Field (51°27'N, 58°16'E), comprising 34 burial mounds attributable to the Orvenni nomadic pastoralists, dated broadly to 900–650 BCE. Full excavation of nine mounds revealed a range of interment types including single primary inhumations in log-lined chambers (n=4), multiple secondary inhumations in stone-cist arrangements (n=3), and one exceptional mound containing a four-horse chariot burial with a presumed elite male interred in articulated pose accompanied by 112 gold plaques, a quiver of 47 bronze arrowheads, and three iron short-swords. Radiocarbon dating of the chariot burial's organic components (wood axle fragments, leather harness elements) converges at 823–798 BCE (2-sigma, n=5). Horse dentition from all excavated mounds (n=84 individuals) indicates slaughter ages predominantly between 4 and 8 years, consistent with breeding-herd management practices. paragraph: The Taroven Highland Project, directed by Dr. Mevlana Shechtr of the Orenburg Institute of Mountain Archaeology, conducted its second full season in 2023 at the Taroven Citadel site (39°48'N, 46°33'E), revealing a fortified hilltop settlement of the Kolvari culture dating to approximately 1300–1050 BCE. Defensive walls, constructed of dry-laid basalt rubble, range from 2.1 to 3.4 m in preserved height and enclose a total area of 1.8 ha. Interior excavation in Sector B uncovered a workshop complex with evidence for both bronze casting (clay mold fragments, n=94; copper slag, 18.6 kg estimated total) and textile production (loom weights, n=213; spindle whorls, n=87). A particular concentration of 16 intact bronze shaft-hole adzes within a single storage context suggests stockpiling of finished goods, possibly for redistribution. Macrobotanical analysis of 34 flotation samples produced 12,340 identifiable seeds, of which rye analogues, barley, and Vicia-type legumes collectively account for 88%. An infant burial in a storage vessel beneath the workshop floor returned a radiocarbon date of 1188–1143 BCE (2-sigma). Geomagnetic prospection of the lower slopes outside the citadel walls suggests an unfortified lower settlement extending a further 3.2 ha, targeted for investigation in the 2025 season.

paragraph: Excavations at Nhelvara Platform Mound (14°22'N, 100°51'E), conducted between 2020 and 2023 under the joint direction of Dr. Preecha Sontavarn (Khoravel University Institute of Art and Archaeology) and Dr. Lenis Vruschek (Central Asian Heritage Consortium), have uncovered a multi-phase ceremonial complex of the Tenvori polity dated approximately 650 BCE–140 CE. The earthen platform, measuring 88 m × 71 m at base and rising 9.6 m, was constructed in at least five discrete building episodes identified by distinct fill-matrix and ceramic horizon sequences. The summit superstructure, a brick-built shrine measuring 14.3 m × 11.8 m, contained a central offering pit 2.1 m deep filled with 312 complete ceramic vessels, 44 bronze bells of varying sizes (range: 4.2–28.7 cm height), and 18 elaborately carved bone tubes interpreted as ritual paraphernalia. Organic residue analysis of 29 ceramic vessels returned signatures of beeswax, plant resins, and a fermented grain beverage. Phosphate soil analysis across the platform summit indicates repeated burning episodes in the northeastern quadrant, consistent with pyrotechnic ritual practice. A 14C date on charred rice from the earliest building episode returned 637–592 BCE (2-sigma). The site represents the largest Tenvori ceremonial monument yet identified and is proposed as a regional cult center under current site-landscape models.

paragraph: The Velthrond Lowlands Survey, a five-year systematic field project (2018–2022) directed by Dr. Heike Flurkenbach of the Ressen Museum für Ur- und Frühgeschichte, covered 340 km² of riverine lowland terrain (52°14'N, 11°38'E), documenting 218 new sites ranging in date from the Mesolithic to the early medieval period. The most extensive Neolithic findspot, designated VLS-Site 44, yielded surface scatters of polished stone axes (n=67), impressed-ware ceramics (n=412 rim sherds), and a dense concentration of grinding stone fragments (n=34 complete or partial pieces). Test excavations at VLS-Site 44 to 1.8 m depth confirmed occupation at 4200–3750 BCE by a community attributed to the Holveni Neolithic horizon, based on radiocarbon dates (n=6) and ceramic typology. A remarkable sub-surface feature at 1.2 m depth consists of a rectangular arrangement of 14 postholes enclosing an area of 11.3 m × 8.7 m, interpreted as a communal long-house with no clear domestic hearth, possibly serving an aggregation or ritual function. Phytolith analysis of floor sediments from the posthole structure identified wheat and barley cultivation alongside wild grass exploitation. Publication of the full survey dataset in the Velthrond Archaeological Monograph Series is anticipated in 2026.

paragraph: A team from the Suranthar Institute of Classical Antiquity, headed by Dr. Ovantha Mire-Bulsek, announced in 2024 the discovery of a previously unknown administrative archive at Kalveth Tell (36°27'N, 38°52'E), within a destruction layer dated to approximately 830 BCE associated with the Survenni expansion into the northern Kalveth plain. The archive comprises 1,144 clay tablets and 37 clay envelope fragments, recovered from a single collapsed storage room measuring 6.4 m × 4.8 m at a depth of 3.1 m below surface. Preliminary examination by epigraphers Dr. Norven Allast and Dr. Brenia Svelk identifies texts in at least two distinct script traditions: a logographic administrative system consistent with the Kalveth bureaucratic corpus and a cursive alphabetic variant, previously attested only at sites 280 km to the west. Tablet contents appear to include grain and livestock ledgers, temple income records, and three narrative texts of unknown function. Associated ceramic assemblage (n=3,470 sherds) is assignable to the Kalveth Late Phase III, bracketed by AMS dates of 863–841 BCE. Metal objects within the archive room include a bronze stylus, six cylinder seals, and 22 bronze weights of standardized form, the heaviest weighing 138.4 g. Full transliteration and translation of the archive is projected to span five years.

paragraph: Fieldwork conducted in 2022 and 2023 at Sornath Dunes (26°38'N, 64°11'E) by the Westport Institute for Arid Zone Archaeology, directed by Dr. Feneva Clarckt, exposed portions of a Late Bronze Age emporium site of the Halverni trade network, active approximately 1400–1100 BCE. Aeolian deflation has exposed architectural remains over an area of approximately 2.6 ha, with mud-brick walls surviving to heights of up to 1.8 m in the more sheltered quadrants. The excavated sector covers 0.4 ha and has revealed a warehouse complex consisting of eight parallel rooms each approximately 18 m in length and 4.5 m in width, connected by a central corridor 2.3 m wide. Contents of Room 4 included 214 intact storage jars of Halverni Type 7b, 42 broken examples of the same type, and 18 cuneiform-adjacent tablets recording outgoing consignments of copper, tin, and aromatic resins. Vessel residue analysis identified grape-derived fermentation products in 37 jars and sesame oil in 29. Imports attested by ceramics and small finds include 54 sherds of a fine painted ware traceable to coastal production centers 900 km to the west and 11 lapis lazuli beads whose geochemical signature is consistent with a highland source 1,200 km to the northeast. The site reinforces current models of extensive long-distance exchange within the Halverni commercial sphere.

paragraph: The Ovallen Cave Project, conducted between 2019 and 2023 under the direction of Dr. Branko Mirselj (Adriatine Institute for Prehistoric Cave Studies), investigated Ovallen Cave (43°51'N, 15°28'E), a complex karstic system with 640 m of documented passages and four distinct occupation loci attributed to the Grelveni hunter-gatherers of the terminal Pleistocene and early Holocene. Excavation of Locus B, the deepest and most extensively occupied chamber, revealed a stratified sequence 2.9 m thick spanning approximately 16,800–9,200 BP based on 17 AMS radiocarbon dates on charcoal and bone. Lithic assemblages from the lower levels (below 1.6 m) include 2,341 retouched pieces dominated by backed bladelets (43%), end-scrapers (22%), and burins (17%), diagnostic of the Grelveni Phase I technocomplex. Faunal remains (n=34,820 identified specimens) are dominated by ibex-analogue cervid and a medium-sized bovid, with fish and bird comprising less than 3% combined. A remarkable engraved limestone plaquette recovered at 1.9 m depth bears incised linear and curvilinear motifs covering both surfaces; portable pXRF analysis of the engraving tool marks is consistent with flint burin use. Parietal art in the adjacent Locus A chamber includes 14 painted animal figures and 22 geometric signs in black pigment; charcoal within the paint matrix returned dates of 15,300–14,900 BP (2-sigma, n=3).

paragraph: Researchers at the Molveth Foundation for Andean Archaeology, led by Dr. Quena Harvist-Salvar, have completed analysis of the Tumba Bravorn cemetery (14°08'S, 75°22'W), a mid-horizon funerary complex of the Quellkari culture, dated 420–680 CE. The cemetery encompasses 187 formally defined grave cuts across a 0.9 ha terrace system on the western slope of the Bravorn ridge, with excavation of 112 contexts completed over four field seasons (2019–2022). Burial form varies by apparent social status: 74 simple pit inhumations contrast with 31 stone-lined cist graves and 7 multi-chamber adobe-brick mausolea each containing 3–9 associated individuals. Textile preservation is exceptional, with 63 intact or near-intact garments recovered, including 14 tapestry-woven panels displaying polychrome geometric registers in camelid fiber with thread counts reaching 100 wefts per cm. Bioarchaeological examination by Dr. Chasvel Terrant recorded cranial modification in 87% of individuals (n=97), with tabular erect and annular forms distributed non-randomly across burial zones, suggesting sub-group affiliation. Stable isotope data (delta-13C, delta-15N, delta-18O) for 58 individuals indicate diet heavily dependent on maize and freshwater protein sources, with three outlier individuals showing isotopic signatures consistent with coastal resource zones 210 km to the west. Radiocarbon dating (n=22) confirms cemetery use between 428 and 674 CE (2-sigma).

paragraph: The Pellavar Delta Landscape Project, a collaborative effort between the Murrenfeld Institute of Geoarchaeology and the Pellavar Cultural Heritage Authority, conducted ground-penetrating radar and targeted test excavations across 180 km² of the Pellavar delta plain (22°31'N, 89°47'E) during 2021–2024. Project director Dr. Helwith Prassen identified 94 subsurface site signatures, of which 22 were ground-truthed by excavation to confirm archaeological deposits. The most significant confirmed site, PDP-44, represents a waterlogged urban precinct of the Benthali riverine civilization, dated approximately 800–600 BCE. Organic preservation is exceptional: a horizontal timber causeway 3.8 m wide and surviving for 46 m in length was exposed at 1.7 m depth below current land surface, with dendrochronological samples pending analysis. Associated wooden artifacts include 14 dug-out canoe fragments, 37 worked posts with mortise joints, and a collection of 112 carved wooden objects including what appear to be votive figurines and measuring rods. Ceramic assemblages from the water-logged deposits include 2,340 sherds dominated by coarse-ware storage vessels, alongside 44 fine red-slipped bowls of the Benthali Prestige Series. Pollen analysis of three sediment cores documents a transition from dense riparian forest to open agricultural land between approximately 950 and 800 BCE, coinciding with the initial phase of Benthali urban development at PDP-44.

paragraph: Dr. Sigvald Ottersven and a team from the Thullmark Archaeological Research Station conducted excavations in 2022 and 2023 at the Gruvholm Bog Site (57°43'N, 12°18'E), recovering a remarkable assemblage of intentionally deposited wooden objects, metalwork, and human skeletal remains associated with the Skalveni Iron Age community, approximately 300 BCE–150 CE. The bog, currently 2.4 m in depth at the excavation locus, yielded 143 wooden objects across six stratified deposition episodes including 34 carved anthropomorphic figurines (mean height: 28.4 cm), 28 wheel-hub fragments, 17 yoke pieces, and one complete four-wheeled wagon frame preserved in articulated condition. Metalwork finds include 88 iron objects (tools, weapons, and fittings), 23 bronze vessels or vessel fragments, and 47 silver items encompassing torcs, arm rings, and coin-like discs. Three preserved human individuals—two adult males and one adult female, confirmed by osteological and aDNA analysis—show evidence of multiple perimortem injuries consistent with sacrificial killing. Radiocarbon dating of the three individuals and associated wooden objects spans 142 BCE–88 CE (2-sigma, n=14 dates), suggesting recurrent depositional practice over more than two centuries. Palaeoenvironmental analysis indicates the bog was an open-water pool at the time of the earliest deposits, becoming increasingly marshy by the period of the latest depositions.

paragraph: The Volvaren Basin Archaeological Initiative, directed by Dr. Carmela Trevisan-Kolb of the Adriatine University Faculty of Prehistory, has documented the Volvaren Copper Age Settlement Complex (45°53'N, 17°44'E), a multi-site cluster spanning approximately 2200–1900 BCE and attributable to the Drevathi horizon. The initiative, now in its third year of fieldwork (2021–2023), has identified 11 contemporaneous open settlements within a 12 km radius, with site areas ranging from 0.4 to 3.8 ha based on surface ceramic distributions. Copper artifacts from Volvaren-3 (n=67) include flat axes, awls, fishhooks, and cruciform pendants; lead isotope analysis of 18 items identifies ore sources consistent with the Krevari massif, 130 km to the northwest. Zooarchaeological analysis demonstrates cattle husbandry predominance (61% of identified specimens), with pig, sheep/goat-analogues, and red-deer-analogue cervid comprising the remainder. The settlement cluster is proposed as a proto-central-place system foreshadowing later Drevathi nucleation.

paragraph: A joint expedition of the Transbaikal Archaeological Consortium and the Irkenveld Heritage Trust, co-directed by Dr. Natalva Torbachen and Dr. Solva Missker, completed a three-season investigation (2020–2022) of the Torvanka River Rock-Art Complex (53°19'N, 108°37'E), comprising 74 individual panels distributed along a 22 km stretch of the Torvanka gorge. The complex, attributable to at least three distinct cultural phases of the Borvenni and Delthark steppe traditions, contains 1,847 catalogued motifs including anthropomorphs (n=312), cervid-like animals (n=584), bovid-like animals (n=371), equids (n=218), and geometric signs (n=362). Superimposition analysis distinguishes a minimum of six sequential phases, with the earliest phase—pecked naturalistic cervid figures averaging 43 cm in height—tentatively associated with the Borvenni Phase I period based on contextual finds at the cliff base (AMS dates: 3400–3100 BCE, n=7). The most recent phase features schematic painted red figures using iron-oxide pigments, yielding uranium-series minimum ages of 870 ± 90 BP at two sampled panels. Portable pXRF of pigments from 34 painted panels identifies manganese oxide (black), iron oxide (red-ochre), and kaolin clay (white), with manganese and iron sources traceable to geological formations within 80 km of the site. A 3D photogrammetric model of the entire complex has been deposited with the Transbaikal Digital Heritage Archive.

paragraph: Excavations at Deltharm Palace Precinct (33°44'N, 35°29'E), conducted from 2021 to 2024 under the direction of Dr. Rovena Khelst of the Levantine Studies Centre, have uncovered a substantial ceremonial quarter of the Belvari urban state, dated approximately 1750–1550 BCE. The precinct, enclosed by a mudbrick temenos wall 2.6 m wide and traceable for 187 m, contains a principal throne-hall complex, two flanking shrines, and an extensive administrative block. The throne hall, 31.4 m × 22.7 m, features a central raised dais platform of lime-plastered mudbrick on which traces of red and yellow pigment survive, interpreted as the decorative surface of a canopied throne setting. Wall plaster fragments from the hall's southern face preserve portions of a figural narrative scene including 14 preserved human figures in procession, rendered in polychrome with lapis blue, cinnabar red, and yellow ochre pigments. Cuneiform-like tablets from the administrative block (n=218, largely intact) are being processed by epigraphers and appear to concern tribute receipts and royal household disbursements. A sealed destruction deposit across the throne hall at 0.8 m depth, including burnt roof timbers dated by AMS to 1562–1541 BCE (2-sigma, n=6), documents an episode of violent destruction coinciding with a period of documented regional instability within the middle Belvari cultural sphere.

paragraph: The Orveld Peninsula Submerged Landscape Project, coordinated by Dr. Ingri Hollenskov of the Nordvik Institute for Marine Heritage, has completed its initial geophysical survey and targeted ROV-assisted sampling across 230 km² of the submerged Orveld shelf (60°11'N, 5°22'E) at water depths of 12–48 m. The survey, using sub-bottom profiling, multibeam bathymetry, and sediment coring, identified 44 potential archaeological anomalies, of which 16 were sampled. Recovered materials include 23 retouched flints, 14 worked bone fragments, 8 hazelnuts with AMS dates clustering at 8700–8400 BP, and fragments of two wooden structures at depths of 34 m and 39 m respectively. The wooden structures, each consisting of stake-and-wattle configurations extending 4–7 m, are associated with organic-rich sediment lenses interpreted as activity surfaces of the Grelleni Mesolithic shore-dwellers. Wood species identification by microscopic anatomical analysis determined willow, alder, and birch in sub-equal proportions. Pollen and diatom assemblages from core samples at two sites indicate freshwater lake conditions at the time of occupation, transitioning to brackish estuarine environments prior to final inundation. The data indicate rapid inundation of this coastal landscape between approximately 8,000 and 7,400 BP, consistent with regional sea-level reconstructions for the early Holocene transgression in the northeastern Orveld basin.

paragraph: Field investigations at Threlkasp Terrace (15°47'S, 47°13'W), a multi-period agricultural landscape in the central Brasoveld plateau region, were completed in 2023 under the direction of Dr. Alondra Turveth of the Brasoveld Institute for Pre-contact Studies. The terrace system encompasses 840 individually mapped agricultural bench-terraces across a 14 km² study area, with stone-faced risers preserved at heights of 0.4–1.9 m. Test excavations in 18 terrace fills identified occupation pottery of the Solventhi planting tradition in 14 units, with radiocarbon dates on associated charcoal spanning 980–1340 CE (2-sigma, n=11), suggesting construction and use over at least 360 years. Soil micromorphology of terrace fill sediments revealed anthropogenic dark earth deposits rich in phytoliths, charcoal fragments, and bone meal, consistent with deliberate soil amendment practices. Maize phytoliths dominate all 18 samples, with tuber-crop evidence present in 11 samples; two samples produced cucurbit phytoliths previously undocumented for Solventhi assemblages. A concentration of ritual deposits—12 ceramic cache vessels, 44 miniature figurines, and 3 polished stone celts—was found at the junction of two major terrace-drainage channels, suggesting hydraulic management points were regarded as spiritually significant by the Solventhi agricultural community. Lidar-derived topographic mapping of the full 14 km² reveals integration of the terrace system with a broader network of sunken field areas and raised-field patches, implying sophisticated landscape-scale water management.

paragraph: The Sulkava Highland Sanctuary, located at 63°08'N, 27°44'E, was investigated between 2022 and 2024 by a team from the Karven Academy for Northern Prehistory led by Dr. Tiina Halvorsken. The sanctuary, identified initially through LiDAR anomaly analysis and subsequently ground-truthed, consists of a central rectilinear enclosure 62 m × 49 m defined by a stone-packed earthen bank 1.8 m wide and 0.7 m in surviving height, with a single formal entrance gap 3.4 m wide facing northeast. Interior features include 14 pit features of varying depth (0.4–2.2 m), a central post setting of 9 postholes in a roughly circular arrangement 6.8 m in diameter, and a concentration of fire-cracked rock covering 18 m² in the southeastern quadrant. Pit fills produced 344 animal bone fragments, predominantly from wild boar-analogue suids (38%) and large cervids (44%), suggesting feasting or offerings; cut-marks on 67 specimens are consistent with meat removal and disarticulation. Ceramic assemblages from pit contexts (n=1,840 sherds) belong to the Sulkava Ware tradition, a regionally distinctive coarse pottery dated by 12 radiocarbon determinations to 780–580 BCE. A small bronze figurine of uncertain zoomorphic form (height 7.2 cm, weight 43.8 g), recovered from the base of the central post setting, is without parallel in the known Sulkava Ware cultural corpus.

paragraph: The 2023 season of the Kharvath Oasis Archaeological Programme, directed by Dr. Merva Tussolt of the Sandveld Institute for Desert Archaeology, focused on the Kharvath East Sector (24°17'N, 56°43'E), revealing a caravanserai complex of the Valtheni merchant community, dated approximately 340–580 CE. The caravanserai plan, exposed over 0.6 ha, comprises a central courtyard 38 m × 33 m surrounded by 22 individual guest-chambers, a stable block for at least 40 animals based on stall partition evidence, a water distribution system with four cisterns, and a small shrine room. The shrine room (4.2 m × 3.7 m) contained a painted plaster program on all four walls, with 14 identifiable figural registers depicting camel caravans, merchant transactions, and religious invocations in the Valtheni decorative idiom; pigment analysis confirmed use of Egyptian blue analogue, malachite, and red ochre. Written materials include 118 wooden writing tablets with ink texts, 34 intact and 84 fragmentary, inscribed in the cursive Valtheni commercial script; content appears to include bills of lading, debt records, and personal correspondence. Faunal analysis of stable deposits identified primarily camel remains (68%), with donkey and horse present in smaller quantities. Radiocarbon dates on the shrine-room painted plaster substrate return a terminus post quem of 338–362 CE (2-sigma).

paragraph: Excavations at Belthavar Promontory Fort (55°38'N, 6°14'W), led by Dr. Oisín Trevelch of the Celtravan Heritage Research Institute, have uncovered an unprecedented sequence of occupations spanning the late Neolithic through Iron Age periods, accumulated within a naturally defensible headland enclosure. The fort's outermost stone rampart, 4.1 m wide at base and surviving to 2.3 m height, encloses an area of 3.4 ha; two inner rampart lines subdivide the interior into distinct functional zones. Radiocarbon dating of 28 samples from wall-construction contexts returns a primary construction date of 920–870 BCE (2-sigma, n=9) for the outermost rampart, with inner ramparts dating to 780–730 and 650–600 BCE respectively. Earlier Neolithic activity is attested by a 2.1 m-deep midden deposit sealed beneath the outermost rampart, yielding 14,230 marine shell fragments, 2,341 animal bones, and 187 sherds of decorated Belthavar Ware, dated to approximately 3400–3100 BCE by 6 AMS dates. Iron Age metalwork from the interior includes 14 iron swords, 44 iron spear tips, 88 bronze horse harness fittings, and a hoard of 23 silver ingots weighing between 18.4 and 47.2 g individually. Stable isotope analysis of cattle bone (n=78) from Iron Age contexts indicates pasturing in coastal maritime environments, consistent with the fort's coastal location and interpreted maritime-raiding economy.

paragraph: The Telvorn Plateau Survey Project, a systematic surface investigation of a 420 km² area in the inner Telvorn highlands (29°44'N, 52°18'E) conducted between 2020 and 2023 by a team from Shahrestan University Institute of Archaeology led by Dr. Fariba Nossrat-Vend, has documented the spatial structure of the Halvari pastoralist landscape, approximately 1500–800 BCE. A total of 312 sites were recorded, ranging from single-artifact findspots to multi-feature complexes; site types include 87 stone-built encampment traces, 44 burial cairns, 34 stone-lined cattle corrals, 28 cupmark rock-art panels, 14 hearth scatters, and 105 isolated lithic concentrations. Systematic shovel-test excavation at 47 sites produced datable material at 34, with radiocarbon dates consistently clustering between 1480 and 820 BCE. The spatial analysis, using Kernel Density Estimation, identifies three higher-density settlement nodes each approximately 15 km in diameter, interpreted as seasonal aggregation foci for otherwise dispersed pastoral groups. A single exceptional site, TPsit-217, occupies a natural rock shelter and contains 2.8 m of stratified sediment; test-trench results indicate occupation spanning 4200–800 BCE, providing a locally anchored chronological sequence against which survey ceramic typologies can be calibrated. Isotopic analysis of caprine bones from TPsit-217 (n=44) demonstrates seasonal altitudinal migration, with winter valley pasturing and summer highland grazing inferred from oxygen isotope profiles.

paragraph: The Solveth River Bridge Settlement (47°31'N, 18°48'E) was brought to light in 2022 during urban infrastructure remediation works and subsequently excavated by Dr. Renata Molváry of the Central Carpathian Archaeological Service over an 18-month emergency season. The site, covering approximately 0.7 ha within a modern urban block, preserves a Late Antiquity–period settlement of the Delvhari federation, dated 380–520 CE by ceramic horizons and 14 AMS radiocarbon dates. Three occupation phases were distinguished in the 0.8–2.4 m stratigraphic sequence; the middle phase (Phase 2, 430–490 CE) produced the greatest artifact density, including 347 iron objects, 112 bronze personal ornaments, 44 bone combs, and 28 glass vessel fragments of at least four distinct vessel forms. A remarkable structure in Phase 2, 14.7 m × 6.3 m, contained evidence for both iron smithing (bloom fragments, tuyère fragments, n=34 slag pieces) and non-ferrous metalworking (crucible fragments, n=18; copper and silver alloy spillages). The spatial co-occurrence of these crafts within a single structure is unusual for the Delvhari settlement tradition. A foundation deposit beneath the structure's main entrance included three deliberately broken bronze brooches, an iron pin, and the skull of a young adult bovid-like animal. The site is interpreted as a craft-specialized node within the broader Solveth River corridor settlement network.

paragraph: The Brelvath Painted Cave system (41°17'N, 1°28'E) has been the subject of a comprehensive re-investigation directed by Dr. Nuria Valserrat-Bloch of the Ibrovian Institute for Quaternary Studies, completed between 2021 and 2024. The cave complex, with 1.2 km of accessible passages, contains 432 catalogued parietal art units including 188 animal figures, 94 geometric signs, 112 hand stencils, and 38 abstract markings. New direct radiocarbon dates on organic pigment components (charcoal-based pigments) from 22 figures return dates ranging from 28,400 ± 320 BP to 14,100 ± 180 BP, demonstrating that the cave was used for image-making across an approximately 14,000-year span by communities of the Grelvani Upper Paleolithic tradition. Uranium-series dating of calcite flowstone overlying or underlying 14 figures provides additional chronological constraints, with a minimum age of 31,200 ± 880 BP for the oldest series of aurochs-analogue bovid figures in the deepest chamber. Portable XRF analysis of 67 painted areas identifies manganese dioxide, iron oxides (hematite and goethite), and charcoal as the principal pigment components; manganese sources are consistent with surface mineral deposits within 35 km. Residue traces of animal fat in four painted areas suggest organic binders or lamp fuel were used during the painting process. The new data substantially revise the previously accepted age range for the Brelvath assemblage and reposition the site as among the oldest decorated cave systems in the Ibrovian peninsula.

paragraph: Archaeological investigations at Tharavorn Lower Terrace (11°22'N, 75°48'E), conducted between 2021 and 2023 under the direction of Dr. Swethan Varuvel of the Kocheri Institute of South Asian Archaeology, have documented a multi-phase megalithic burial ground attributable to the Halveni Iron Age tradition, dated approximately 900–300 BCE. The terrace, measuring 320 m × 190 m, contains 147 megalithic monuments including dolmenoid structures (n=44), cist graves (n=67), stone-circle burials (n=22), and cairns (n=14). Systematic excavation of 28 monuments revealed human skeletal remains in 21 contexts, with a minimum number of 83 individuals represented; multiple interments per monument are common, with one stone-circle burial containing elements of at least 9 individuals of varied ages. Grave goods include iron objects (tools and ornaments, n=312 total), black-and-red ware ceramics (n=844 vessels or vessel fragments), and 34 gold ear ornaments of the wire-loop type. Radiocarbon dates from 18 monuments span 888–318 BCE (2-sigma), with the dolmenoid and cairn types clustering earlier and cist graves clustering later in the sequence, suggesting a diachronic shift in mortuary practice. Osteological analysis by Dr. Priya Selvendra identified evidence for secondary burial rites in 14 monuments, where disarticulated bones had been sorted and redeposited. Portable XRF of the gold ear ornaments indicates alloy compositions of 78–94% gold with silver and minor copper.

paragraph: The Morveldt Harbour Excavation Project, directed by Dr. Lena Hartvekke (Skagen Research Foundation for Maritime Antiquity), conducted its fourth and fifth field seasons in 2022 and 2023 at the Morveldt medieval harbour site (56°29'N, 10°12'E), recovering evidence for a flourishing late medieval emporium of the Balthoveni seafarers, approximately 1180–1380 CE. Waterlogged conditions at 0.6–2.8 m depth beneath the modern harbour infill have preserved a remarkable suite of organic materials including 14 partial ship timbers, 88 wooden quayside piles with tool-mark evidence, 312 leather shoe fragments representing at least 74 individual shoes, 44 complete wooden vessels (bowls, buckets, tubs), and 22 wicker basket fragments. Ceramic assemblages from five stratified phases (n=18,400 sherds total) document the evolution of the site's trade connections, with imports from coastal production centres to the north accounting for 28% of Phase 5 ceramics compared with only 6% in Phase 1. paragraph: Dr. Yusuf Altinkara of the Anatolie Institute for Bronze Age Studies led the 2022–2024 excavation campaign at Görveltepe Höyük (38°54'N, 35°17'E), a 14 m-high multi-period mound attributable to successive cultures of the central plateau, with primary investigation of the Late Bronze Age levels, ca. 1380–1180 BCE. Excavation in the central sounding to 7.3 m below mound surface exposed seven stratigraphic horizons, of which Horizons III–V (depths 3.1–5.8 m) correspond to the principal Late Bronze Age occupation. Horizon IV, the best-preserved, yielded a palatial building complex covering 0.18 ha, with 2.4 m-thick mudbrick walls, polished lime-plaster floors, and a roofed storeroom containing 88 pithoi of standardized capacity (each approximately 180 L by volume). An associated tablet archive of 74 clay tablets was found in a collapsed wooden chest; texts, in the Vorthun administrative script, include census data, ration lists, and two letters referencing a king named Atvarshan and inter-dynastic correspondence. Anthropomorphic figurines of terracotta (n=112) from Horizon IV votive contexts represent the largest such assemblage from any single Bronze Age site in the plateau region. AMS radiocarbon dates on short-lived charcoal from Horizon IV cluster at 1278–1241 BCE (2-sigma, n=8), while the destruction sequence of Horizon III is dated to 1194–1162 BCE.

paragraph: Investigations at the Velskarn Iron Smelting Complex (59°52'N, 17°31'E), conducted between 2019 and 2023 by the Uppsveld Institute for Economic Archaeology under the direction of Dr. Bodvar Sigridtsen, have documented the largest known iron production facility of the Thorveni culture, dated approximately 200–550 CE. The complex covers a minimum of 4.3 ha and contains at least 34 smelting furnace bases, 18 roasting-pit features, 7 slag-heap accumulations with a combined estimated volume of 3,200 m³, and 3 residential structures interpreted as housing for specialist iron workers. Total slag mass across all heaps is estimated at 1,440 metric tonnes, implying cumulative iron production of approximately 200–360 metric tonnes over the facility's operational lifespan. Furnace construction varies by phase: early furnaces (Phase 1, 200–350 CE) are small pit furnaces with clay-and-stone linings 0.8–1.1 m in diameter, while Phase 2 furnaces (350–550 CE) are larger shaft furnaces with reconstructed heights of 1.4–1.9 m. Ore sourcing by portable XRF and lead isotope analysis of 67 slag samples identifies four distinct ore supply zones, the most distant 87 km from the site, implying organized regional ore procurement. Bloom iron and finished tools recovered from residential structures indicate on-site secondary smithing in addition to primary smelting. A coin assemblage of 28 items spans the full operational period and includes issues from four distinct minting authorities.

paragraph: The Kavarna Plateau LiDAR Survey, covering 780 km² of highland terrain (42°31'N, 25°17'E) and conducted in 2022–2023 under the direction of Dr. Vanya Storveth of the Plovdevi Institute for Remote Sensing in Archaeology, identified 612 previously undocumented archaeological features, fundamentally revising understanding of the Thraveni prehistoric landscape. A concentration of 34 burial mounds in the northern sector, forming a spatially discrete cemetery landscape, produced radiocarbon dates in test excavations spanning 1600–1200 BCE, attributable to the early Thraveni Bronze Age. Two enclosure systems with internal circular structures are without regional parallel and may represent a non-Thraveni cultural component; ceramic assemblages from test trenches are inconsistent with any known local tradition and are under specialist review. The 112 mapped trackway segments, when analyzed as a network, connect the elevated plateau with six river-valley crossing points, suggesting a well-organized transhumance or regional exchange route system. Full LiDAR data and field verification records have been submitted to the National Heritage Database of Thravenia.

paragraph: A salvage excavation at Bolvensk Station (55°46'N, 37°28'E), conducted in 2023 under the direction of Dr. Alexei Gruzevsk of the Central Russoveld Heritage Institute, uncovered a merchant's warehouse quarter of the Drelkovi urban tradition, dated approximately 1020–1180 CE, sealed beneath 1.8–2.6 m of later medieval overburden. paragraph: Dr. Thembeka Oluvarn of the Witsveld Institute for African Archaeology led a 2021–2023 investigation at Khoramba Enclosure (18°53'S, 31°47'E), a dry-stone walled settlement complex attributable to the Halveth cattle-keeping tradition, dated approximately 900–1250 CE. The enclosure, with an outer perimeter wall 2.8 m wide and 1.4 m in surviving height enclosing 3.1 ha, contains 22 subsidiary enclosures of varying size and 14 circular stone-built hut platforms. Excavation of four hut platforms and six enclosure fills produced a total of 8,340 ceramic sherds, of which the Halveth stamped-ware diagnostic type accounts for 34%; four non-local ceramic types, present in low frequencies (1–3%), suggest exchange with communities up to 400 km distant. Faunal assemblages (n=14,220 identified specimens) are dominated by domesticated cattle (74%), with sheep-goat-analogues (18%), domestic fowl-analogues (5%), and wild fauna (3%) also present; cattle mortality profiles indicate a herd-management strategy emphasizing dairy production over meat offtake. A midden deposit in the central enclosure area yielded 14 iron hoes, 22 iron rings, 34 glass beads of at least 7 color types, and 8 copper alloy wire bracelets. Radiocarbon dates on cattle bone collagen (n=14) span 912–1238 CE (2-sigma), confirming site use over at least three centuries. Phosphate analysis indicates the central enclosure was used continuously as a cattle kraal throughout the site's occupation.

paragraph: The Halvarn Deep Cut Excavation, a large-scale research project at Halvarn Tell (35°12'N, 36°34'E) co-directed by Dr. Sophie Mertens-Tollver (Leuvaldt University Institute of Archaeology) and Dr. Jaris Enscott (Harveth College, Near Eastern Languages), has now completed its sixth season (2023), reaching a maximum depth of 9.4 m below mound summit in the central sounding. The sequence reveals continuous occupation from approximately 4500 BCE to 650 CE, with the Chalcolithic levels (9.4–7.8 m, approximately 4500–3800 BCE) representing the earliest settlement phase. Chalcolithic deposits contain chaff-tempered pottery with incised chevron decoration, obsidian blade technology using pressure-flaked prismatic cores, and evidence for sheep-goat-analogue herding and emmer-wheat cultivation. A mud-brick platform structure at 8.9 m depth, 6.7 m × 5.3 m, with no domestic assemblage, is interpreted as a proto-communal storage or ritual facility. The Early Bronze Age levels (7.8–5.1 m, approximately 3200–2400 BCE) produced the densest occupation evidence, including three superimposed courtyard-house neighborhoods and a kiln complex for mass-producing standardized storage vessels; over 2,400 complete or near-complete vessels from these levels are currently being studied. An administrative archive of 33 clay tablets in the Halvarn proto-literate script, recovered from a 3400 BCE destruction context at 7.1 m depth, is the earliest such archive from the central Halvarn plain region.

paragraph: Excavations at Quenvar Burial Platform (6°14'S, 80°33'W), a tiered earthen monument on the Quenvar desert coast, were completed in 2024 by the Coastal Heritage Research Group, directed by Dr. Marta Varellanos-Chu of the Lambayequi Regional University. The monument, measuring 94 m × 72 m at base and 14.2 m in height, was constructed in four episodes spanning approximately 1800–700 BCE, attributable to the Valkheni coastal fishing and farming tradition. Excavation of a central burial shaft, 11.7 m deep, exposed 23 superimposed burial offerings including intact textile bundles (n=14, many containing wrapped human mummy bundles), copper and gold sheet ornaments (n=212 pieces), and 88 ceramic vessels of the Valkheni Fine Polychrome type. The lowermost offering, at 11.2 m, contained 7 complete human mummy bundles, two of which preserve extraordinary textile outer wrappings with 144 distinct color zones achieved through warp-patterning techniques; fiber analysis confirmed camelid (88%) and bast fiber (12%) composition. Radiocarbon dates on internal organic materials from the two lowest offerings cluster at 1783–1724 BCE (2-sigma, n=4). Stable nitrogen isotopes of mummy hair samples (delta-15N: 14.2–16.8 per mil) indicate a predominantly marine-protein diet for all 23 sampled individuals. The find represents the most intact stratified offering sequence yet recovered from a Valkheni monumental burial context.

paragraph: The Horveld Lakeside Settlement (52°08'N, 21°14'E), a waterlogged pile-dwelling site of the Drevali Neolithic horizon, was excavated between 2020 and 2023 by Dr. Konstanty Breszevk of the Varsoveld Archaeological Research Centre. The settlement, constructed on artificial wooden platforms over a shallow embayment of the former Horveld Lake, is preserved beneath 1.8–3.4 m of lake sediment accumulated since site abandonment circa 3000 BCE. Excavation of two 10 m × 5 m trenches exposed a complex of wooden platforms, pile foundations (n=347 piles, mean diameter 12.4 cm), plank walkways, and two complete house structures with preserved walls standing to 0.9 m height. Finds assemblages include 4,340 ceramic sherds, 88 polished stone axes, 34 antler tools, 14 wooden paddles or oars (three complete), 23 fragments of woven basketry, and 88 pieces of plaited rush matting. An extraordinary concentration of 312 wooden objects in the western trench includes 14 turned wooden cups (lathe-turning is debated as a technique at this date), agricultural tool handles, and two decorated cylindrical objects of uncertain function with incised herringbone motifs. Dendrochronological analysis of 44 structural piles returned felling dates concentrated at 3,288–3,274 BCE, with a secondary episode at 3,241–3,237 BCE, suggesting construction in two closely spaced events. Zooarchaeological analysis confirms a mixed economy of cattle herding, pig keeping, fishing, and hunting of wild taxa.

paragraph: The Drenova Citadel Project, directed by Dr. Raluca Muresán of the Transylvania University Institute of Prehistory, concluded its three-season investigation (2021–2023) at Drenova Hill (46°02'N, 23°33'E), an Iron Age fortified settlement of the Corvathi culture, dated approximately 650–350 BCE. The citadel, crowning a natural limestone spur at an elevation of 842 m, is enclosed by three concentric rampart lines with combined wall mass estimated at 18,400 m³ of stone and earth construction. The inner citadel (0.8 ha) contained a principal building of 18.4 m × 11.7 m, interpreted as an elite residential and administrative structure based on differential artifact distributions: 44 imported fine-ware bowls, 22 iron swords, 14 bronze vessels, and 88 amber objects were recovered from the western wing, while the eastern wing produced evidence for textile manufacture (loom weights, n=187) and food processing (quern stones, n=22). A dedicatory hoard sealed beneath the building's threshold slab comprised 34 gold items including torcs, bracelets, and a sheet-gold beaker weighing 228.4 g. Radiocarbon dates on short-lived charred grains from primary floor contexts cluster at 598–542 BCE (2-sigma, n=7). The outer enclosure (3.6 ha) was occupied primarily by standardized rectangular structures interpreted as housing for retainer or garrison populations. Spatial analysis identifies a clear social hierarchy within the citadel layout consistent with ranked Corvathi chiefly governance.

paragraph: Researchers from the Molveth Pacific Archaeology Institute, led by Dr. Keoni Halvethson, completed excavations in 2023 at Taravoa Ridge (17°43'S, 149°27'W), a hilltop ceremonial complex of the Aoreni island polity, dated approximately 680–1050 CE. The complex includes seven stone-faced platform temples (marae-analogues) arranged along a 340 m linear axis oriented 14° west of north, with the principal temple measuring 38 m × 22 m at its base and rising 4.8 m above surrounding grade. Radiocarbon dates on human bone collagen (n=9) span 682–741 CE (2-sigma), documenting the construction-phase interments. A secondary occupation of the temple summit, dated 920–1050 CE by 7 AMS dates, is associated with extensive marine faunal deposits dominated by large tuna-analogue fish and turtle bone, suggesting large-scale feasting events. An assemblage of 88 complete and fragmentary ceramic vessels from the secondary occupation includes 22 examples of a red-slipped pedestal-bowl type previously documented only in the western Aoreni archipelago, 1,200 km distant, implying long-distance inter-island interaction during the late phase. Petrographic analysis of 34 ceramic sherds confirms non-local temper mineralogy.

paragraph: The Salveth Valley Paleolithic Survey, conducted between 2018 and 2023 under the direction of Dr. Maren Drevholtz of the Rhenish Institute for Quaternary Prehistory, has systematically documented the Middle Paleolithic occupation of a 60 km-long river valley system (50°37'N, 7°14'E), recording 34 sites ranging from open-air assemblage scatters to rockshelter deposits. The most productive locality, Salveth Rockshelter (SR-12), yielded 8.4 m of stratified deposits spanning approximately 120,000–38,000 BP based on optically stimulated luminescence (OSL) dating of 22 sediment samples. Lithic assemblages from SR-12 (n=14,820 retouched pieces and 44,230 unretouched debitage items) demonstrate a transition from Levallois-dominant technology (lower 4.2 m) to a discoidal-dominant technology (upper 4.2 m) dated to approximately 65,000–55,000 BP, representing the clearest documented technological shift within the regional Grelveni Mousterian-analogue sequence. Faunal assemblages from SR-12 (n=28,340 identified specimens) are dominated by woolly rhinoceros-analogue perissodactyls (32%), cave bear-analogue ursids (22%), and reindeer-analogue cervids (28%), with cut-mark frequencies indicating intensive carcass processing. Three human tooth fragments recovered from SR-12 at depths corresponding to OSL ages of 88,000–92,000 BP are being subjected to ancient DNA analysis; preliminary morphological assessment is ambiguous regarding taxonomic affinity. Publication of the full SR-12 sequence is in preparation.

paragraph: The Bolvari Sacred Precinct (31°48'N, 35°52'E) was investigated during three field seasons (2021–2023) by a joint team from the Solvendt University Biblical and Near Eastern Studies Department and the Koreth Institute for Iron Age Levant, co-directed by Dr. Hana Brennthal and Dr. Soren Elvkist. Excavations exposed a sequence of three superimposed shrine complexes spanning approximately 1050–600 BCE, representing the best-preserved sacred precinct of the Bolvari cultic tradition yet excavated. The earliest shrine (Phase 1, ca. 1050–900 BCE) consists of a single-room structure 8.7 m × 6.4 m with a raised altar platform of mudbrick, before which a deposit of 312 sheep-goat-analogue mandibles was found in situ. Phase 2 (ca. 900–750 BCE) expanded the sacred area to include a courtyard 28 m × 21 m and a tripartite temple building 14.4 m × 9.8 m; votive deposits in the courtyard include 44 bronze figurines, 88 faience amulets, 14 ivory inlays, and 2,340 ceramic offering vessels. Phase 3 (ca. 750–600 BCE) saw further enlargement and the addition of a standing-stone installation of 7 basalt stelae, the largest 2.1 m in height, within the courtyard. A cache of 23 silver ingots totaling 487 g was deposited beneath the threshold of the Phase 3 temple entrance. Radiocarbon dates on burned organic material (n=18) confirm and refine the ceramic-based phasing throughout the sequence.

paragraph: A team from the Oroveld Institute for Agricultural Origins, led by Dr. Bremena Thulskar, conducted targeted excavation at Golveth Terrace Site (36°31'N, 36°52'E) in 2022–2023, focusing on the earliest evidence for plant cultivation within the Halveni pre-pottery Neolithic horizon, dated approximately 9800–8500 BCE. Excavation of a 4 m × 8 m trench to 3.1 m depth encountered 11 archaeological layers, of which the lowest 4 (layers 8–11, depths 2.1–3.1 m) predate any pottery. Macrobotanical analysis of 78 flotation samples from these layers by Dr. Fenne Clovssen identified 44,320 seed remains, with wild-type einkorn-analogue wheat (with rachis morphology intermediate between wild and domesticated forms) accounting for 38%, alongside wild-type lentil-analogues, wild grass seeds of 14 additional species, and acorn cotyledon fragments. The ratio of wild to transitional cereal morphotypes shifts significantly between Layers 11–9 (predominantly wild) and Layer 8 (predominantly transitional), dated to approximately 9300–9100 BCE by 8 AMS dates on single seeds. Phytolith analysis identifies 23 additional cultivated plant types in Layer 8 absent from Layers 9–11. Storage pit features in Layer 8 (n=6), with fills dominated by the transitional cereal seeds, are consistent with in-settlement grain processing and short-term storage. These findings place Golveth Terrace among the earliest sites demonstrating incipient plant domestication in the northern Halveni arc.

paragraph: Dr. Yukino Rashverth of the Tokai Institute for Historical Archaeology directed the 2022–2024 investigation of Mishelvara Port Town (34°41'N, 137°02'E), a medieval commercial settlement of the Halveni maritime tradition dated approximately 1080–1320 CE. The site, partially submerged under tidal flats, was excavated using a combined approach of terrestrial trenching in the landward portions and underwater grid excavation in 1–3 m of water for the seaward zones. Terrestrial excavations (14 trenches, total area 420 m²) exposed warehouse complexes, a market street 4.8 m wide surfaced with fired-brick pavers, and a religious precinct comprising a shrine building and two subsidiary structures. The shrine building (12.4 m × 8.8 m) contained 312 intact ceramic offering vessels, 44 bronze ritual bells, and a cache of 14 lacquerware items of which 9 preserve figural decoration. Underwater excavations recovered 1,840 ceramic sherds (88% locally produced utilitarian wares; 12% imported fine wares from three non-local production centers), 44 iron anchors and anchor components, and 28 wooden structural elements from the port quayside. Dendrochronological sampling of 11 quayside timbers returned felling dates spanning 1094–1138 CE. A lead seal assemblage (n=67) from the market street bears merchant and guild marks in four distinct script traditions, indicating a cosmopolitan commercial community. Radiocarbon dates (n=22) confirm continuous occupation from approximately 1082 to 1318 CE.

paragraph: The Vethland Chalcolithic Cemetery Project, directed by Dr. Cornelia Altmann-Voss of the Dorthelm Institute for Prehistoric Social Studies, completed analysis of the Vethland East Burial Ground (51°29'N, 13°22'E) in 2024, following three excavation seasons (2020–2022). The cemetery, spanning 1.4 ha, contains 234 inhumation burials attributable to the Vethland Chalcolithic horizon, dated 3300–2800 BCE by 34 AMS radiocarbon measurements. Burial rite is predominantly single extended inhumation in oval pit graves, but 22 burials involve multiple individuals (2–5 per grave), and 14 graves contain skeletal elements only—disarticulated deposits interpreted as secondary burials. Grave good distributions reveal strong patterning by biological sex and estimated age-at-death: adult males (n=88) receive copper daggers (mean 1.4 per burial), stone battle-axes (mean 0.8 per burial), and boar-tusk plaques; adult females (n=94) receive copper rings, clay spindle whorls, and shell bead strings; children (n=32) receive amber beads and miniature ceramic vessels in proportions scaled to estimated age. A statistically exceptional burial (Burial 44) of an adult male contained 23 copper items, 14 gold discs, 88 amber beads, and a complete set of archery equipment, representing the highest artifact-value grave in the cemetery by a factor of 4. Strontium isotope analysis (87Sr/86Sr) of tooth enamel from 88 individuals identifies 12 non-locals, including Burial 44's occupant (87Sr/86Sr: 0.7134), pointing to the interment of individuals from diverse geographic origins.

paragraph: A major synthetic publication from the Selvorna Basin Archaeological Project, directed by Dr. Marike Holtveld of the Amberveld University Centre for Landscape Archaeology, presents the results of a decade-long interdisciplinary investigation (2013–2023) of a 520 km² catchment area in the Selvorna uplands (44°18'N, 20°37'E). The project combined systematic field survey, 144 targeted test excavations, 22 larger open-area excavations, extensive palaeoenvironmental coring, and LiDAR-based geomorphological mapping to reconstruct 12,000 years of human-environment interaction within the basin. Key findings include the identification of 14 superimposed agricultural field systems spanning the Neolithic through the medieval period, demonstrating near-continuous agricultural exploitation interrupted only by a 300-year hiatus (approximately 500–800 CE) corroborated by pollen evidence for woodland regeneration. The Bronze Age occupation (approximately 2200–900 BCE) is represented by 34 settlement sites of varying size and function, integrated within a hierarchical settlement system whose apex site, Selvorna Central Mound, covers 8.4 ha and contains evidence for administrative, craft-production, and ceremonial activities. The Iron Age (900–100 BCE) shows a significant shift toward smaller, nucleated hilltop settlements (n=22 identified), each enclosing 0.4–2.8 ha, interpreted as the material signature of increased territorial competition within the Delvhari cultural sphere. Palynological records from 12 sediment cores document deforestation episodes, cereal pollen expansions, and erosional events that correspond closely with identified archaeological transitions, providing a high-resolution proxy record for human land-use change across the full 12,000-year sequence.
